svg.text: LIBRAS
NOTATION — SOUTH AMERICAN SIGN LANGUAGES
LIBRAS: LINGUA BRASILEIRA DE SINAIS
LIBRAS: sign language of Brazil's Deaf community; ~3M users
Among the 10 largest sign languages in the world by user count
Origins: 1857, Rio de Janeiro, via French Sign Language (LSF)
LIBRAS MANUAL ALPHABET
LIBRAS: one-handed manual alphabet (adapted from LSF via Portugal)
26 handshapes for Portuguese alphabet letters; includes nasal vowels
Accented letters (a with tilde, o with tilde): specific handshapes
SUTTON SIGNWRITING IN BRAZIL
Brazil: one of countries with most active SignWriting use
LIBRAS written with SignWriting in Deaf schools and some publications
Sign-Wiki: online LIBRAS dictionary with video + SignWriting
LIBRAS RECOGNITION
2002: Brazilian law 10.436 recognizes LIBRAS as official language
2005: Decree 5626 mandates LIBRAS interpreters in federal services
LSA · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
LSA: LENGUA DE SENAS ARGENTINA
LSA: ~90,000 users in Argentina; Buenos Aires-centered
Founded: 1857, Buenos Aires (same year as LIBRAS in Rio!)
Spanish and French missionaries brought sign methods to Argentina
LSA MANUAL ALPHABET
LSA: one-handed manual alphabet for Spanish letters
Includes ene (ñ): separate handshape for Spanish nasal palatal
Based on Spanish fingerspelling tradition from LSE (Spanish SL)
NOTATION COMPARISON
Both: one-handed manual alphabets (shared LSF influence)
LIBRAS: Portuguese alphabet • LSA: Spanish alphabet (similar base)
Both: no universal written standard; video primary
LIBRAS: SignWriting use growing • LSA: glossing predominates in research
PLATE 66a ALT · LIBRAS / LSA · NOTATION
